title: Cat Passing Bloody Urine Drops: Urgent Care Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is passing small drops of bloody urine, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary triage is required.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat passes only small drops of urine accompanied by blood (hematuria), it is a clinical sign of Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD). This condition involves intense inflammation of the bladder or a physical obstruction in the urethra. The cat is often straining (stranguria) and may appear to be constipated, though the issue is actually urinary.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: High. This is considered a medical emergency. While it may be a severe urinary tract infection or sterile cystitis, it is frequently a sign of a life-threatening urethral blockage, which is especially common in male cats. A cat that cannot urinate will experience a rapid buildup of toxins and potassium in the blood, leading to kidney failure or heart failure within 24 to 48 hours.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear photo of the urine droplets on the floor or in the litter box can provide vital information to your veterinary team. The color, clarity, and exact volume of the discharge help the vet determine the severity of the inflammation and whether the cat is partially or fully obstructed before they even begin a physical exam.

faqs.question: Can I wait until tomorrow to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because urinary blockages can become fatal so quickly, any cat straining to urinate should be seen by an emergency veterinarian immediately.

faqs.question: Is this more common in male or female cats?
faqs.answer: While both can suffer from urinary issues, male cats are at a significantly higher risk for life-threatening obstructions due to their longer, narrower urethras.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a complete blockage?
faqs.answer: Signs include frequent trips to the litter box with no result, crying out in pain, licking the genital area excessively, hiding, and vomiting.
